label: schultes::ayahuasca_maoi_dmt
text: Ayahuasca — Banisteriopsis caapi + Psychotria viridis. Schultes (1967, 1979); Davis (1996); Plotkin (1993). Multiple Amazonian nations: Shipibo, Shuar, Siona-Secoya, Tukano. The combination: caapi vine (MAO-inhibiting beta-carbolines: harmaline, harmine) + chacruna leaves (DMT). Schultes: 'The probability of this specific combination being discovered by chance approaches zero — it implies systematic pharmacological experimentation over generations.' DMT is orally inactive due to MAO destruction in gut; beta-carbolines inhibit MAO, allowing oral DMT activity. Most sophisticated phytopharmacological discovery in any pre-industrial tradition. Current research: Phase 2 trials at Imperial College London, Johns Hopkins, NYU — multiple showing remarkable efficacy for treatment-resistant depression and PTSD. FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for psilocybin (related compound); ayahuasca trials ongoing.

label: schultes::curare_surgical
text: Curare — Strychnos toxifera / Chondrodendron tomentosum. Schultes (1941-1953 Amazon expeditions). Yanomami, Makushi, Wapishana, Waorani. Preparation documented: specialist knowledge of which vines, which parts, boiling duration, potency testing (timing paralysis of test animals) — 3-5 day process. Amazonian hunters empirically identified the remarkable selectivity: skeletal muscle paralysed, cardiac and smooth muscle unaffected at therapeutic doses. This selectivity — tubocurarine's specific action at peripheral nicotinic receptors vs cardiac muscarinic receptors — required decades of 20th century research to explain. Harold Griffith introduced to surgery (1942). All subsequent neuromuscular blockers (vecuronium, rocuronium, atracurium) are synthetic descendants. Without curare-derived compounds, modern surgical procedures as currently practiced would be impossible.

label: schultes::plant_hallucinogens_97species
text: Amazonian Plant Hallucinogens — Schultes & Hofmann, Plants of the Gods (1979). Schultes documented 97 plant species used as hallucinogens by indigenous Amazonian peoples — vs only 15 known from the Old World. Key plants: (1) Virola calophylla bark resin (5-MeO-DMT) — 14 Amazonian nations; (2) Brugmansia (tree datura) — used across South America for surgical anesthesia, vision, warrior preparation — same genus as Aztec Toloache, convergent discovery across the Americas; (3) Yopo/Anadenanthera peregrina (bufotenin snuff) — Caribbean to Brazil. Schultes: 'The cultures of the Amazon appear to have systematically sought and catalogued psychoactive plants with thoroughness found nowhere else. The healer's primary role is to navigate between worlds using plants as vehicles.' These 97 documented psychoactive plants represent the largest pharmacological dataset of altered-consciousness medicine in world history.

label: schultes::poison_medicine_spectrum
text: The Poison-Medicine Spectrum in Amazonian Ethnobotany. Schultes field analysis. A consistent finding: no sharp boundary between medicine and poison — same plant used as weapon, medicine, and vision-inducer depending entirely on dose and preparation. Documented examples: (1) Curare — arrow poison at high dose, surgical muscle relaxant at low dose; (2) Brugmansia — fatal at high dose, anaesthetic at intermediate, visionary at low; (3) Caapi — emetic at high dose, vision-inducing at medium, sedative at low; (4) Strychnos — cardiac poison at high dose, heart tonic at low (precursor to cardiac glycoside pharmacology). The Amazonian healer's mastery of this dose-response spectrum, achieved through empirical experimentation across generations, represents applied pharmacology that Western medicine only systematised after receptor theory development in the 20th century.

label: plotkin::traditional_healers_amazonian
text: Amazonian Healer Role — Plotkin, Tales of a Shaman's Apprentice (1993); Schultes field notes. Healer terms: pajé, curandero, vegetalista. Role documentation: (1) 5-10 year apprenticeship — plant preparation, ceremony, song (icaros), spiritual diagnosis simultaneously; (2) Dietary restriction during training — specific foods and sexual abstinence to establish 'plant spirit' relationship; (3) Diagnostic framework — illness as intrusion (foreign spirit), soul loss, or sorcery — each requiring different treatment; (4) Treatment integration — plant medicine given alongside icaro songs considered essential to direct the medicine. Plotkin documented among Tirio of Suriname: 79 separate plant preparations for 65 disease categories. Yanomami: over 200 plant species with documented medical use. The convergent integration of spiritual diagnosis, ceremony, and plant medicine across all Amazonian traditions parallels exactly the priest-physician integration documented by Cobo for the Inca, Badianus for the Aztecs, and Tibetan physician-priests — a universal pre-industrial medical principle.

label: schultes::coca_amazonian
text: Erythroxylum coca — Coca (Amazonian uses distinct from Andean). Schultes documented coca use by 36 distinct Amazonian peoples. Amazonian documentation distinct from Andean (Cobo): primarily used for energy, hunger suppression, and ceremony rather than altitude-specific applications. Interesting pharmacological observation: Amazonian coca preparations typically less alkaloid-potent than Andean — different species, different soil, different preparation. This parallels variation in therapeutic potency documented in Kampo (Japanese vs Chinese Atractylodes) and represents evidence of traditional pharmacognosy — distinguishing quality variations within a species. The 36-nation documentation demonstrates that coca knowledge spread across the Amazon basin from the Andean origin zone through empirical adoption, with each group adapting it to their specific needs.

label: schultes::rubber_wound_medicine
text: Hevea brasiliensis — Rubber Tree, Seringa. Schultes Amazon expeditions 1941-1953. Traditional uses documented: fresh latex applied to wounds as antiseptic barrier and wound closure; bark decoction for fever; roots for edema. Mechanism of latex wound dressing: forms flexible, breathable, antimicrobial seal; latex proteins have antimicrobial activity; physical barrier prevents contamination. Schultes: 'Indigenous use of rubber latex as wound dressing predates the commercial rubber industry by millennia.' This represents pre-industrial biomedical engineering — using a natural polymer for medical wound management. Economic significance: Hevea brasiliensis became the basis of the global rubber industry. Like curare and ayahuasca, this represents unique Amazonian chemical knowledge becoming the basis of global commercial applications with no compensation to source communities.
